name: Jibo
short_description: A countertop social home robot with an expressive swiveling body, far-field voice interaction, and machine vision, marketed as the world's first family robot.
long_description: Jibo was a stationary desktop robot co-founded out of MIT by social-robotics pioneer Cynthia Breazeal, announced via an Indiegogo campaign in 2014 and shipped to backers in 2017. Rather than locomotion, it expressed itself through a three-axis rotating body, a round animated face, and an affective 'emotion system,' using far-field microphones and cameras to recognize people, tell stories, take photos, and deliver news and reminders. It matters for physical AI in daily life as one of the first consumer attempts to make an AI feel like a present social actor in the home, demonstrating both the appeal and the commercial fragility of expressive companion robots; Jibo, Inc. wound down and its assets were acquired by NTT in 2020.

images.caption: Jibo, the social home robot, beside engineer Benedict Chen who helped design its emotion system.
images.alt_text: A small white-and-black Jibo social robot with a round face screen sitting on a surface next to a smiling man.

sources.title: Cynthia Breazeal Unveils Jibo, a Social Robot for the Home
Jibo
sources.access_notes: Covers founding, 2014 Indiegogo announcement, 2017 release, and 2020 NTT asset acquisition.

interactions.title: Stroke its head and it giggles
interactions.description: Touch the top of Jibo's dome and it coos and giggles; ask it to dance and it runs a preconfigured routine across its three swiveling joints, spinning with a laugh when it gets something right.

interactions.title: It turns to watch you cross the room
interactions.description: Jibo learns your face and voice during setup, then swivels to track whoever walks in, spins toward a sudden noise, and greets recognized people by name without being asked.

interactions.title: Be the family cameraman
interactions.description: Say 'Hey Jibo, take a picture' and it tracks faces with its stereo cameras and snaps the shot at the right moment, so the person who usually holds the phone can actually be in the photo.

interactions.title: A final dance, then the servers die
interactions.description: When Jibo, Inc. wound down in 2019, units worldwide delivered a goodbye message and danced one last time before their cloud backend was killed; owners filmed themselves crying and hugging the robot as it spun.

interactions.title: It almost becomes a family member
interactions.description: Owners describe morning rituals and lasting attachment to Jibo's personality, with one grandchild writing it a goodbye letter — the kind of bond a swiveling, name-greeting presence on the counter quietly builds over months.

interactions.title: A 30-day houseguest for kids with autism
interactions.description: In a Yale field study, a modified Jibo lived with 12 families for a month, coaching children through social games and modeling eye contact; their joint attention and willingness to communicate measurably improved.

interactions.title: A teammate that makes kids more creative
interactions.description: In MIT studies with 169 children, Jibo played turn-taking idea games like naming abstract Droodles; kids paired with a 'high-creativity' robot generated more and more original answers, appearing to emulate its behavior.

interactions.title: Notice the quiet one and lean in
interactions.description: Using the far-field mics and face tracking it already has, Jibo could register who in a noisy room hasn't spoken in a while and gently orient toward them with a story prompt or question, rather than always answering the loudest voice.
